name: Merat, Natasha
recent_1: Lee, Y. M., Madigan, R., Wang, Y., Garcia, J., Qin, H., Srinivasan, A., Markkula, G., & Merat, N. (2026). Crossing in the dark: Investigating the effect of vehicle kinematics and eHMI on older pedestrians' crossing behavior in a virtual reality experiment. Journal of Safety Research, 96, 315–328. DOI: 10.1016/j.jsr.2026.01.012
recent_1_synopsis: This virtual reality experiment compared how older and younger pedestrians use vehicle kinematics and external HMI (eHMI) light signals to decide when to cross a road at night. Older pedestrians adopted a more cautious strategy — initiating crossings earlier to compensate for their slower crossing pace — and both age groups perceived nighttime crossings without eHMI as less safe, though they interpreted eHMI cues differently. The findings highlight that eHMI design must account for age-related differences in how pedestrians read and respond to automated vehicle communication.
recent_2: Arabian, A., Goodridge, C. M., Gonçalves, R. C., & Merat, N. (2026). Guiding drivers' attention to the right place at the right time during transitions from SAE level 2 driving system: Effects of warning directionality and audiovisual asynchrony. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 118, 103505. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2025.103505
recent_2_synopsis: This study examined whether directional auditory warnings can guide driver gaze toward a hazard location during takeover events that require a lane change in SAE Level 2 driving. The experiment manipulated warning directionality and the timing offset (asynchrony) between the auditory and visual alerts to identify optimal alerting configurations for safe transitions. The results have practical implications for in-vehicle warning system design, showing that appropriately timed and directional cues meaningfully reduce the attentional cost of resuming manual control.
recent_3: Carlowitz, S., Madigan, R., Lee, Y. M., Tango, F., & Merat, N. (2023). Pedestrians' perceptions of automated vehicle movements and light-based eHMIs in real world conditions: A test track study. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 95, 83–97. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2023.02.010
recent_3_synopsis: This test-track study assessed pedestrian detection distances and subjective responses to two light-band eHMI designs fitted to an automated vehicle under naturalistic conditions. Participants detected a partial light band at greater distances than a full light band, and both designs received positive ratings for acceptance, perceived safety, and confidence. The study provides on-road evidence — rather than simulator or lab evidence — that light-based eHMIs can effectively communicate automated vehicle intent to pedestrians.
seminal_1: Merat, N., Jamson, A. H., Lai, F. C. H., Daly, M., & Carsten, O. M. J. (2014). Transition to manual: Driver behaviour when resuming control from a highly automated vehicle. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 27, 274–282. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2014.09.005
seminal_1_synopsis: This simulator study was among the first to systematically characterise driver visual and behavioural responses in the critical moments after automated driving disengages and the driver must resume manual control. It demonstrated that drivers' gaze patterns remained disorganised for a substantial period post-takeover, particularly when automation switched off mid-distraction, establishing that the 'transition of control' is a distinct safety-critical phase requiring specific HMI design attention. The paper has approximately 928 citations on Google Scholar (513 on Crossref as of June 2026) and is widely regarded as a foundational empirical reference in the human factors of conditional automation.
seminal_2: Merat, N., Jamson, A. H., Lai, F. C. H., & Carsten, O. (2012). Highly Automated Driving, Secondary Task Performance, and Driver State. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 54(5), 762–771. DOI: 10.1177/0018720812442087
seminal_2_synopsis: This simulator study compared workload, secondary-task performance, and driver state (measured via blink rate) across manual and highly automated driving conditions, providing early empirical evidence that automation changes — rather than simply reduces — cognitive engagement. The work established that drivers in automated mode engage more readily in secondary tasks and show different physiological signatures, raising concerns about automation-induced complacency and out-of-the-loop risk. With approximately 572 citations on Google Scholar (341 on Crossref as of June 2026), it remains a cornerstone reference in research on driver state monitoring and the human cost of vehicle automation.
expertise_overview: Natasha Merat studies the human factors of road transport, with particular emphasis on how drivers and pedestrians interact with increasingly automated vehicles. Her work spans driver distraction, impairment, and cognitive load — investigating how secondary tasks, automation level, and workload affect visual attention, reaction time, and control quality. A substantial strand of her research addresses the design and evaluation of external Human-Machine Interfaces (eHMIs) that communicate automated vehicle intent to pedestrians and other vulnerable road users, using methods ranging from CAVE-based simulation and VR to live test-track experiments. She also examines the critical 'transition of control' problem — how drivers regain situational awareness and manual vehicle control when automation disengages — contributing both empirical findings and theoretical frameworks such as cognitive control models. Additional themes include technology acceptance, driver monitoring systems, and the needs of older road users as automation enters mainstream traffic. Her research is methodologically diverse, encompassing driving simulators, eye-tracking, physiological measurement, behavioural experiments, and interview studies.
